title: Shared-state and cancellation safety

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Shared-state and cancellation safety; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: The Python Standard Library; Python 3.14; asyncio, concurrent.futures, threading, multiprocessing, queue, and synchronization documentation; atomic topic: Shared-state and cancellation safety
sources.claim: Concurrency safety protects complete invariants across shared-state transitions and guarantees cleanup during failure or cancellation, preferably through confinement, immutable messages, and structured ownership.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Concurrency safety protects complete invariants across shared-state transitions and guarantees cleanup during failure or cancellation, preferably through confinement, immutable messages, and structured ownership.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A check-then-update balance operation is placed under one lock or owned by one task rather than relying on two individually safe dictionary operations. Your goal is to repair a teaching example with unsafe shared state or lost cleanup. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Typing, tests, logging and debugging; Generators and lazy pipelines; asyncio coroutines and tasks; Threads and processes; Context managers. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Concurrency safety protects complete invariants across shared-state transitions and guarantees cleanup during failure or cancellation, preferably through confinement, immutable messages, and structured ownership. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: A single operation's atomic implementation does not protect multi-step logic, locks can deadlock, cancellation can arrive at await points, finally blocks can also fail, and queues need shutdown/backpressure policy. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Built-in collection operations make every multi-step shared-state algorithm atomic and cancellation can only occur between function calls.

includes: Recognize races, protect invariants and guarantee cleanup across failure or cancellation.
Repair a teaching example with unsafe shared state or lost cleanup.
excludes: A single operation's atomic implementation does not protect multi-step logic, locks can deadlock, cancellation can arrive at await points, finally blocks can also fail, and queues need shutdown/backpressure policy.

outcomes: Repair a teaching example with unsafe shared state or lost cleanup.

examples.title: Shared-state and cancellation safety: direct language case
examples.situation: A check-then-update balance operation is placed under one lock or owned by one task rather than relying on two individually safe dictionary operations.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A check-then-update balance operation is placed under one lock or owned by one task rather than relying on two individually safe dictionary operations.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Protect the entire invariant transition, minimize shared mutation, and place resource release in cancellation-safe context or finally paths.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Protect the entire invariant transition, minimize shared mutation, and place resource release in cancellation-safe context or finally paths.

examples.title: Shared-state and cancellation safety: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: An async worker releases its semaphore in an async context/finally path when cancellation interrupts the awaited operation.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: An async worker releases its semaphore in an async context/finally path when cancellation interrupts the awaited operation.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: A single operation's atomic implementation does not protect multi-step logic, locks can deadlock, cancellation can arrive at await points, finally blocks can also fail, and queues need shutdown/backpressure policy.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Built-in collection operations make every multi-step shared-state algorithm atomic and cancellation can only occur between function calls.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Repair a teaching example with unsafe shared state or lost cleanup.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Built-in collection operations make every multi-step shared-state algorithm atomic and cancellation can only occur between function calls.

analogy.example: a coordination desk that overlaps waiting jobs, assigns CPU work to separate rooms, and protects complete shared transitions can introduce Shared-state and cancellation safety, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: concurrency models, asyncio, threads, processes, cancellation, and safety
limitations: the event loop, GIL, serialization, atomicity, scheduling, and cancellation points must be reasoned about technically
